ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for runner bean casserole with pork; closest_parent 'braised dish (food product)' used as the nearest defensible category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Legume vegetables, cooked) used as closest_parent; no specific code exists for runner bean braised with pork in casserole.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific mixed dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical shared serving of runner bean casserole with pork is approximately 200 g, representing a side dish portion for one person in a Chinese family-style meal.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried green beans with pork / Braised green beans with pork — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (runner beans cooked, pork loin cooked) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Chinese mixed vegetable-meat dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 砂锅扁豆丝 (runner bean casserole with pork) found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values estimated from weighted combination of: (1) USDA SR Legacy item 11053 'Beans, snap, green, cooked, boiled' for runner bean component (~60% by weight), (2) USDA SR Legacy item 10062 'Pork, loin, whole, cooked, roasted' for pork component (~30% by weight), and (3) standard seasoning allowance (soy sauce, oil, garlic, ginger, ~10% by weight). Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for this braised dish. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of green beans and mixed vegetable-meat dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked green beans GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for 砂锅扁豆丝 (runner bean casserole with pork). GI estimated at 32 based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked green beans / snap beans (GI ~30–38, low category). The addition of pork and braising liquid does not materially raise the GI of the dish. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 12.8) / 100 ≈ 4.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 640 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, this dish produces a low glycemic load (~4.1 per serving) and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal with controlled carbohydrate portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork and sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 1.6 g saturated fat and 320 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and prefer lean pork cuts to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Runner Bean Casserole with Pork (砂锅扁豆丝, Shā Guō Biǎn Dòu Sī) is a Chinese braised dish consisting of hand-sliced runner bean strips cooked together with pork in a clay or ceramic casserole pot. The dish is a staple of home-style Chinese cooking and is popular in Singapore Chinese restaurants. Runner beans are sliced into thin strips to maximise surface area and flavour absorption, then braised slowly with pork, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and aromatics until tender and fragrant. The result is a savoury, slightly umami dish with a soft yet slightly firm texture from the beans and a rich, meaty depth from the pork. The casserole method retains heat and moisture, producing a concentrated flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~7.2 g/100 g) from pork, dietary fibre (~2.4 g/100 g) from runner beans, and a low glycemic index (~32), making it a relatively balanced side dish. Sodium content is moderate to high (~320 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets due to the pork content. It is typically served as a shared side dish alongside steamed rice in a Chinese family-style meal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component data (runner beans ~60%, pork ~30%, seasoning ~10%) using USDA SR Legacy approved sources. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked green beans) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy sources used for this dish estimation. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked pork and green bean preparations per USDA SR Legacy data. The food_name has been normalised from the input menu string '砂锅扁豆丝 Runner Bean Casserole' to the canonical English form 'Runner Bean Casserole with Pork' to reflect the pork component described in the dish description. The original Chinese name 砂锅扁豆丝 is preserved in local_names and aliases. This dish contains pork and is not halal or suitable for vegetarian/vegan diets. Sodium content is a moderate concern for hypertension management. GI estimated at 32 based on University of Sydney GI Database cooked green beans analogue; glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as (32 × 12.8 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 4.1.
